title: The Midnight Caller
description: A late-night radio host is found dead in a locked studio. The last call came in at midnight. The killer was already in the room.

scenes.text:
The clock on Studio B's wall reads 12:03 AM. The red ON AIR light is still glowing, casting a blood-warm glow across the room.

Mark Devlin — host of WKRP's late-night phenomenon 'The Midnight Hour' — is slumped over the mixing board. A single bullet wound in the back of his head. His coffee mug, still warm, rests beside an open logbook.

The studio door is locked from the inside. The deadbolt is engaged. The window — third floor, fire escape side — is bolted shut.

The building's night security guard found him at 12:07 AM during his rounds. He had to use his master key to get in.

'Impossible,' the detective mutters. 'A locked room. No witnesses. But someone pulled that trigger. Find out who.'

Forensics places the time of death between 11:45 PM and 12:00 AM. A single 9mm round, close range, execution-style. No shell casing found — the killer took it with them. Mark was sitting when he died. No signs of a struggle.
scenes.clue_detail: Time of death: 11:45 PM - 12:00 AM. Single gunshot to the back of the head at close range. No shell casing recovered. No signs of struggle.

scenes.text:
Patricia Vance, the station manager, is pacing the hallway when you arrive. Late 50s, crisp blazer, the kind of woman who's seen everything in this business.

'Mark was difficult,' she admits. 'But he was our star. The ratings were through the roof.'

She tells you Mark locked the studio door every broadcast. 'Privacy, he said. Better for the intimate vibe.' Tonight was supposed to be different — he had a guest scheduled. A woman. She called in sick at the last minute. Mark decided to go solo.

'Who was the guest?' you ask.

Patricia lights a cigarette. Her hands are shaking. 'I don't know. Mark handled his own bookings.'

Mark's cell phone records reveal three events in the final minutes of his life:

11:52 PM — Incoming call from a blocked number. Lasted 4 minutes 23 seconds.
11:57 PM — He called his voicemail. Hung up after 12 seconds.
11:58 PM — A text message arrived from the same blocked number.

The text, recovered from his phone, reads: 'I know what you did. And I have proof. Meet me at the station. Midnight. Or everyone finds out.'
scenes.clue_detail: Blocked number called at 11:52 PM (4m 23s). Text at 11:58 PM: 'I know what you did. And I have proof. Meet me at the station. Midnight. Or everyone finds out.'

scenes.text: Four people had reason to want Mark Devlin dead. The station manager who had something to hide. The morning show host whose career was dying. The ex-wife who was drowning in unpaid alimony. And the mysterious caller who called every single night — until Mark humiliated him on air.

The station is quiet now. Yellow crime scene tape cross-hatches the hallway. Somewhere, a janitor's radio plays static.

You walk the building. Patricia's office on the second floor has a window overlooking the parking lot. Danny Cross's booth is on the first floor — he does the morning show from here. No connection to Studio B except the stairwell.

Eleanor Devlin's parking ticket puts her two blocks away at 11:55 PM. She says she was nearby but never went inside.

And the Night Owl? Security saw a man in a hoodie loitering outside the station at 11:30 PM. He disappeared before anyone could approach.

The detective looks at you. 'Someone's lying. Everyone's lying.'

The backup audio recording reveals something crucial. At 11:58:32 PM, the audio cuts to static for 17 seconds. When it returns, the recording continues, but forensic analysis of the static gap reveals:

A low-frequency thump at 11:58:33 — consistent with a gunshot, muffled by a pillow or cloth.
A metallic scrape — a chair being pushed back.
Two distinct sets of footsteps: one heavy (men's boots), one lighter (women's heels).

The door clicks open and closed at 11:58:50.
scenes.clue_detail: Backup audio has 17-second static gap at 11:58:32 PM. Inside: a muffled gunshot, a chair scrape, and TWO sets of footsteps (heavy boots + light heels). Door opens and closes at 11:58:50.

scenes.text:
The door's deadbolt requires a key from either side. The key is still in the lock on the INSIDE. The gap under the door is barely a quarter-inch — too small to reach through.

But above the door, the ventilation grille catches your eye. Four screws — and one is freshly scratched. You remove the grille. The duct is just wide enough for a hand to reach through and twist the deadbolt from outside.

Someone studied this room. Someone who knew the layout, the lock, the schedule. Someone who'd been in this studio before.
scenes.clue_detail: Deadbolt key found in inside lock. Ventilation grille above door has one freshly scratched screw. Duct is wide enough to reach through and lock the deadbolt from outside. Killer had intimate knowledge of the studio.

scenes.text: You've gathered the evidence. The blocked number. The two sets of footsteps. The vent trick. The studio knowledge. Who killed Mark Devlin?

suspects.name: Patricia Vance
suspects.motive: Mark was threatening to leave for a rival network and had been digging into the station's finances. He found something damaging.
suspects.alibi: Security log shows she swiped into her second-floor office at 10:15 PM and never left until the guard called her. Her office camera confirms she was there the whole time.
suspects.suspicious_detail: She turned pale when asked about the blocked number. Her hands shook when lighting a cigarette. But security footage gives her an airtight alibi — she never left the second floor.

suspects.name: Danny Cross
suspects.motive: Mark's midnight slot was gaining traction while Danny's morning show was hemorrhaging listeners. Management was considering giving Mark the prime morning slot.
suspects.alibi: At The Rusty Mic, three blocks away. Credit card transaction at 11:47 PM. Bartender confirms he was there until 12:15 AM.
suspects.suspicious_detail: A brass button from his jacket was found near the studio door. He claims he 'lost it days ago,' but it was clean — no dirt, no wear. Almost like it was recently removed and placed there.

suspects.name: Eleanor Devlin
suspects.motive: Mark owed her over $40,000 in back alimony. Checks kept bouncing. She was about to take him to court again.
suspects.alibi: Says she was home asleep. Her daughter confirms she was home at 11 PM, but the daughter's room is upstairs and wouldn't hear a car start.
suspects.suspicious_detail: A parking ticket on her dash from a lot two blocks from the station — timestamped 11:55 PM. She admits she was nearby but claims she 'chickened out and drove home.'

suspects.name: The Night Owl
suspects.motive: Mark mocked him on air two weeks ago. The caller left an unsettling voicemail: 'You'll regret making me a laughingstock.' He called every single night — until he stopped, exactly one week ago.
suspects.alibi: Unknown. No one has ever seen his face. He only ever called in.
suspects.suspicious_detail: A man in a hoodie was seen loitering outside the station at 11:30 PM, then vanished. But the blocked-number text was sophisticated — not the work of an angry fan. The killer knew the station's layout, its locks, its vent system. This wasn't a stranger.

scenes.text: The killer was the Night Owl.
scenes.explanation:
The Night Owl was never a random fan. He was Gary Mitchell, 41 — the station's former overnight engineer, fired six months ago after Mark complained about his work.

Gary knew the studio better than anyone. He built that mixing board. He installed those vents. He knew exactly which screws were loose and which duct would give him access to the deadbolt.

The blocked number was a burner phone. The text was bait to keep Mark alone in the studio after his guest cancelled — a cancellation Gary orchestrated by posing as the guest's agent.

Gary slipped in through the fire escape while Mark was distracted by the text message. He used a pillow to muffle the shot, took the casing, and escaped through the vent access — the same way he'd locked the door behind him.

The two sets of footsteps on the backup recording? His heavy work boots — and Mark's office chair rolling back when he stood up to read the text message.

His motive? Revenge. Mark got him fired. His wife left him. He lost everything. And in Gary's mind, Mark Devlin had to pay.

When police arrived at Gary's apartment, they found a shrine to Mark Devlin — newspaper clippings, station memorabilia, and a 9mm pistol with one round fired. Ballistics confirmed the match.

The perfect locked-room murder — undone by one thing the killer forgot: the backup audio system had been recording the whole time.
